turns.content: I've been using fat LTO in CMake before, so I thought setting lto = true in Cargo.toml would give me the same aggressive cross-crate inlining that flattens everything into a single monolithic object file.

That's actually a common mistake because Rust's cargo uses thin LTO by default even when you set lto = true. Thin LTO performs optimizations within crates but doesn't fully merge them like fat LTO does, so cross-crate inlining remains limited compared to what CMake produces with fat LTO flags.
But I tested it and my binaries are still growing larger than expected. Is there a way to force Rust to behave like CMake's fat LTO without manually managing LLVM bitcode files?

You can achieve more aggressive optimization by using lto = "thin" in Cargo.toml combined with the linker flag -C link-lto, but even then Rust defaults to thin LTO which trades binary size for compile speed. To get true fat LTO behavior without external tools requires enabling full cross-crate inlining through specific compiler flags that go beyond standard cargo configuration.
